title: try / catch / finally
instruction:
When code might fail, wrap it in `try`. Catch specific exceptions in `catch`. `finally` runs ALWAYS — whether an exception happened or not.

Run this — see what happens with a bad index and a good index.
expectedOutput: Error at 3: Index 3 out of bounds for length 3
successMessage: finally ran every single time — success or exception. Use finally for cleanup: closing files, database connections, network sockets. The new try-with-resources (coming in step 6) does this automatically.

title: Checked vs unchecked exceptions
instruction:
**Checked exceptions** (e.g. `IOException`, `SQLException`) — must be caught or declared with `throws`.
**Unchecked exceptions** (e.g. `NullPointerException`, `IllegalArgumentException`) — no requirement to handle.

The hierarchy: `Throwable → Exception → RuntimeException (unchecked)`, `Exception (non-Runtime) = checked`.
expectedOutput: IOException: Not a text file: config.xml
successMessage: readFile declares 'throws IOException' — callers are forced to handle it at compile time. process throws IllegalArgumentException (unchecked) — callers can handle it or let it propagate.

title: Multi-catch
successMessage: Each exception type caught and handled differently. Order matters — put more specific exceptions first. Catching Exception at the end is a last resort catch-all.

title: Custom exception classes
successMessage: A custom exception with extra data (shortfall). The catch block can access e.getShortfall() — more information than a generic exception. Custom exceptions make error handling meaningful.

title: try-with-resources
successMessage: close() was called automatically — even without a finally block. This is AutoCloseable / try-with-resources. It's the correct way to handle files, database connections, and HTTP clients.

title: Build a robust input parser
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Write `parseAge(String input)` that:
- Throws `NullPointerException` (with message "Input cannot be null") if input is null
- Throws `NumberFormatException` if input isn't a number (Integer.parseInt does this automatically)
- Throws `IllegalArgumentException` (with message "Age must be 0-150") if outside valid range
- Returns the parsed age otherwise

Test it with: `null`, `"abc"`, `"-5"`, `"200"`, `"25"`.
hint: if (input == null) throw new NullPointerException("..."); int age = Integer.parseInt(input); if (age < 0 || age > 150) throw new IllegalArgumentException("...");
successMessage: A validated input parser with meaningful error messages. Each failure mode is distinguishable. This is how production APIs validate user input. Chapter 15 done!
